section.title: The Presentation and the Boy Jesus
section.sectionRationale: Luke rounds off the infancy narrative with two temple scenes. First the infant Jesus is brought to Jerusalem to be presented to the Lord, where the aged Simeon takes Him up and sings that his eyes have seen God's salvation, a light for the Gentiles and glory for Israel, and where the prophetess Anna gives thanks. Then, twelve years later, the boy Jesus is found in the temple among the teachers, and speaks His first recorded words: I had to be in My Father's house. It is one movement about the child growing under the Law yet already marked as the Lord's own Son.
section.thesis: The child brought in poverty to the temple is God's own salvation, a light for the nations and glory for Israel, appointed for the fall and rising of many, so that even as He grows in obedience and wisdom, He is already about His Father's business.
section.frameNote: Seven beats: Jesus presented at the temple; Simeon's song of salvation; Simeon's prophecy to Mary; the thanksgiving of Anna; the return to Nazareth; the boy Jesus in His Father's house; and His growth in favor with God and man.

units.label: Jesus presented at the temple
units.note: according to the Law of Moses

verses.text: And when the time of purification according to the Law of Moses was complete, His parents brought Him to Jerusalem to present Him to the Lord
(as it is written in the Law of the Lord: “Every firstborn male shall be consecrated to the Lord”),
and to offer the sacrifice specified in the Law of the Lord: “A pair of turtledoves or two young pigeons.”

units.label: Simeon's song of salvation
units.note: my eyes have seen Your salvation

verses.text: Now there was a man in Jerusalem named Simeon, who was righteous and devout. He was waiting for the {{paraklesis|consolation}} of Israel, and the Holy Spirit was upon him.
The Holy Spirit had revealed to him that he would not see death before he had seen the Lord’s Christ.
Led by the Spirit, he went into the temple courts. And when the parents brought in the child Jesus to do for Him what was customary under the Law,
Simeon took Him in his arms and blessed God, saying:
“{{despotes|Sovereign Lord}}, as You have promised, You now dismiss Your servant in peace.
For my eyes have seen Your {{soterion|salvation}},
which You have prepared in the sight of all people,
a {{phos|light}} for revelation to the Gentiles, and for glory to Your people Israel.”

units.label: Simeon's prophecy to Mary
units.note: a sword will pierce your soul

verses.text: The Child’s father and mother were amazed at what was spoken about Him.
Then Simeon blessed them and said to His mother Mary: “Behold, this Child is appointed to cause the rise and fall of many in Israel, and to be a sign that will be spoken against,
so that the thoughts of many hearts will be revealed, and a sword will pierce your soul as well.”
There was also a prophetess named Anna, the daughter of Phanuel, of the tribe of Asher, who was well along in years. She had been married for seven years,
and then was a widow to the age of eighty-four. She never left the temple, but worshiped night and day, fasting and praying.
Coming forward at that moment, she gave thanks to God and spoke about the Child to all who were waiting for the redemption of Jerusalem.

units.label: The return to Nazareth
units.note: the grace of God was upon Him

verses.text: When Jesus’ parents had done everything required by the Law of the Lord, they returned to Galilee, to their own town of Nazareth.
And the Child grew and became strong. He was filled with wisdom, and the grace of God was upon Him.

units.label: The boy Jesus in His Father's house
units.note: did you not know that I had to be in My Father's house

verses.text: Every year His parents went to Jerusalem for the Feast of the Passover.
And when He was twelve years old, they went up according to the custom of the Feast.
When those days were over and they were returning home, the boy Jesus remained behind in Jerusalem, but His parents were unaware He had stayed.
Assuming He was in their company, they traveled on for a day before they began to look for Him among their relatives and friends.
When they could not find Him, they returned to Jerusalem to search for Him.
Finally, after three days they found Him in the temple courts, sitting among the teachers, listening to them and asking them questions.
And all who heard Him were astounded at His understanding and His answers.
When His parents saw Him, they were astonished. “Child, why have You done this to us?” His mother asked. “Your father and I have been anxiously searching for You.”
“Why were you looking for Me?” He asked. “Did you not know that I had to be in My Father’s house?”
But they did not understand the statement He was making to them.

units.label: Growing in favor with God and man

verses.text: Then He went down to Nazareth with them and was obedient to them. But His mother treasured up all these things in her heart.
And Jesus grew in {{sophia|wisdom}} and stature, and in favor with God and man.

paraklesis.gloss: Consolation, comfort, encouragement. Simeon was waiting for the consolation of Israel, a way of naming the messianic hope drawn from Isaiah's Comfort, comfort my people. The comfort God's people longed for arrives in the child Simeon holds.

despotes.gloss: Sovereign master, the owner with full authority over a household or servant, from which the English despot comes. Simeon addresses God as the Master who has complete right over his life, ready now to release His servant in peace.

soterion.gloss: Salvation, the saving act or the means of salvation. Simeon says his eyes have seen God's salvation, and he is looking at a baby. Salvation has a face and a name, prepared in the sight of all peoples.

phos.gloss: Light. The child is a light for revelation to the Gentiles and glory to Israel, echoing Isaiah's servant given as a light to the nations. Luke's Gospel widens from Israel to all peoples, and here that reach is already announced.

sophia.gloss: Wisdom. Jesus grew in wisdom and stature and in favor with God and man. Truly human, He develops and matures, yet already at twelve His understanding astounds the teachers, for the wisdom of God is at home in Him.

basic.context: Obeying the Law of Moses, Joseph and Mary bring the infant Jesus to the temple to present Him to the Lord and offer the sacrifice of the poor, a pair of birds. There the devout old Simeon, promised he would see the Messiah, takes the child in his arms and sings that his eyes have seen God's salvation, a light for the Gentiles and glory for Israel. He warns Mary that this child will divide many and that a sword will pierce her own soul. The aged prophetess Anna gives thanks and speaks of Him to all awaiting redemption. Years later, at twelve, Jesus stays behind in the temple, and when His anxious parents find Him, He asks why they searched, since He had to be in His Father's house. He returns to Nazareth, obedient and growing in wisdom.
basic.hermeneutics: Luke shows Jesus fully under the Law, brought up in a devout, poor family, yet already recognized by Spirit-led witnesses as God's salvation for the world. The two temple scenes bracket His childhood: at both, the temple is His Father's house, and both point beyond Israel to all peoples. The key questions are who this child is, why His coming brings both rising and falling and a piercing sword, and what His first words reveal about His identity.
basic.originalLanguages: Simeon waits for the paraklesis, the consolation, of Israel, and addresses God as despotes, Sovereign Master. His eyes have seen God's soterion, His salvation, a phos, a light, for the Gentiles and glory for Israel. Years later the boy Jesus grows in sophia, wisdom, and in favor with God and man.
basic.typology: The offering of the poor and the presentation of the firstborn place Jesus under the Law He came to fulfill. Simeon's song draws directly on Isaiah's servant, the light to the nations. The temple, called His Father's house, and the redemption of Jerusalem point to the greater redemption He will accomplish.
basic.christ: Simeon names the baby as God's own prepared salvation and the light of the world. At twelve, Jesus calls the temple His Father's house, the first hint in Luke of His unique divine sonship. He is the fulfillment both witnesses had waited for.
basic.god: God keeps His promise to Simeon and honors the patient devotion of Anna. He prepares His salvation in the sight of all peoples, reaching Gentile and Jew alike. He is faithful to the humble who wait for Him.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 42 and 49 give the servant as a light to the nations. Leviticus 12 sets out the purification offering. Malachi 3 speaks of the Lord suddenly coming to His temple. And John 2 shows Jesus again zealous for His Father's house.
basic.oneStory: The infancy narrative closes with salvation held in an old man's arms. The child under the Law is the light for the nations and the glory of Israel, destined to divide and to be pierced. Even as a boy He is about His Father's business, and the whole story now waits for Him to grow up and go to Jerusalem.

context.literary: These scenes complete Luke's diptych of infancy, and the fourth of his great songs, the Nunc Dimittis of Simeon, joins the Magnificat, Benedictus, and Gloria. The two temple episodes, the presentation and the twelve-year-old, frame Jesus' hidden years and both center on the temple, preparing for a Gospel that will move steadily toward Jerusalem. Verse 52 forms a growth summary matching verse 40.
context.historical: The Law required purification after childbirth and the redemption of the firstborn. The offering of two birds rather than a lamb was the provision for the poor, marking the family's low means. Boys neared the age of religious responsibility around twelve, and Passover drew crowds to Jerusalem who traveled in large groups, which explains how Jesus could be missed for a day.
context.canonical: Simeon's song universalizes the hope of Israel, echoing Isaiah's servant songs and announcing salvation for the Gentiles, a theme Luke carries into Acts and to the ends of the earth. The piercing sword foreshadows the passion. Jesus calling the temple His Father's house anticipates the fuller revelation of His sonship and His later confrontation with the temple, binding this scene into the whole sweep of Scripture.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show Jesus faithfully brought up under the Law in a humble, devout home, to record the Spirit-led testimony of Simeon and Anna that He is God's salvation for all peoples, and through the temple episode to give the first glimpse of His unique consciousness as the Son of His Father.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The rituals of purification and firstborn redemption belonged to the Law and are not binding on believers now. What endures is the truth of who Jesus is, the model of patient, prayerful waiting for God seen in Simeon and Anna, and the call to treasure and seek understanding as Mary did.

debates.question: What is the sword that will pierce Mary's soul?

views.label: Her grief at the cross
views.case: Most read it as the anguish Mary will feel as she watches her Son rejected and crucified, a personal sorrow bound up with the opposition He provokes.

views.label: The dividing judgment He brings
views.case: Coupled with the thoughts of many hearts being revealed, some see the sword as the discriminating effect of Christ, which cuts even through Mary as part of Israel; the two senses are not exclusive.

debates.bearing: Either way it warns that this salvation comes through suffering and division, and even those closest to Jesus are not spared the cost of following Him.

debates.question: What does the boy Jesus mean by My Father's house or business?

views.label: The temple as His Father's house
views.case: The most natural sense is that He belongs in the temple, God's house, showing at twelve a settled awareness that God is His Father in a unique way.

views.label: His Father's business or affairs
views.case: The footnote allows I had to be about My Father's business, a slightly wider sense of being occupied with God's concerns; both point to His unique relationship to God as Father.

debates.bearing: Both readings reveal the first hint of Jesus' divine sonship and His sense of a mission from His Father that even His parents did not yet grasp.

study.originalLanguages: Simeon has been waiting for the paraklesis, the consolation of Israel, a phrase soaked in Isaiah's Comfort, comfort my people, the messianic hope of a nation longing to be comforted. Taking the baby, he addresses God as despotes, the Sovereign Master who holds full authority over His servant's life and may now dismiss him in peace. His eyes, he says, have seen God's soterion, His salvation, and he is holding an infant, for salvation now has a face. That salvation is a phos, a light, for revelation to the Gentiles and glory to Israel, echoing the servant of Isaiah given as a light to the nations. And when the story leaps ahead twelve years, Luke says the boy grew in sophia, wisdom, and in favor with God and man, truly human and maturing, yet so full of understanding that the teachers are astounded.

typology.fulfillment: Isaiah's servant given as a light for the Gentiles is fulfilled in Jesus, whom Simeon names a light for revelation to the Gentiles, Isaiah 49:6 and Luke 2:32.

typology.type: The firstborn presented to the Lord
typology.fulfillment: The consecration of the firstborn under the Law points to Christ, the firstborn who is truly holy to the Lord and gives Himself for His people, Exodus 13:2 and Luke 2:23.

typology.type: The Lord coming to His temple
typology.fulfillment: Malachi's promise that the Lord would suddenly come to His temple is fulfilled as the child, and later the boy, is brought into His Father's house, Malachi 3:1.

study.christ: Simeon, holding a poor couple's baby, declares that his eyes have seen God's salvation, prepared in the sight of all peoples, a light for the Gentiles and the glory of Israel. In one aged man's song the whole world's hope is gathered into a single child. Simeon also foretells that this Savior will divide Israel and be opposed, and that a sword will pierce His mother, the first shadow of the cross. Twelve years on, Jesus' first recorded words, I had to be in My Father's house, reveal a settled awareness that God is His Father in a unique way. He is the salvation of God, truly human, growing and obedient, yet already the Son about His Father's business.
study.god: God is faithful to those who wait for Him. He keeps His promise to Simeon that he would see the Christ before he died, and He honors Anna's decades of fasting and prayer with the sight of the Redeemer. He prepares His salvation openly, in the sight of all peoples, reaching beyond Israel to the Gentiles. He works through a humble, law-keeping family of slender means, and He is present in His temple, which His Son calls His Father's house. The God of this passage rewards patient, prayerful devotion and widens His mercy to the whole world.

crossReferences.note: The servant given as a light for the nations, that God's salvation may reach the ends of the earth, quoted in Simeon's song.
The purification offering after childbirth, with the provision of two birds for those too poor to bring a lamb, behind verses 22 to 24.
The Lord whom you seek will suddenly come to His temple, fulfilled as the child is brought in and the boy sits among the teachers.
Jesus again claims the temple as His Father's house, consumed with zeal for it, echoing Luke 2:49.

study.oneStory: The infancy narrative closes with God's salvation cradled in an old man's arms and proclaimed to all who wait for redemption. The child under the Law is the light for the nations and the glory of Israel, appointed to divide and destined to be pierced. Even as a boy He is drawn to His Father's house, aware of a mission His parents cannot yet grasp. The stage is set: this Son will grow, and the Gospel will follow Him on the long road to Jerusalem, where the sword and the salvation meet.

sermon.title: Nunc Dimittis
sermon.why: Begg expounds Luke 2:29-32, Simeon's song at the presentation, showing how this faithful man embraced Jesus as his peace, the glory of Israel, and the light of the world. A faithful, expository handling of the heart of this movement, from a sermon that reads Luke 2:22-32 together.

quiz.q: What did the offering of two birds indicate about Jesus' family?

options.text: They were poor, since two birds were the provision for those who could not afford a lamb.
options.note: Leviticus 12 allowed the poor to bring birds instead of a lamb, marking the family's humble means.

options.text: They were carefully obeying the Law of Moses.
options.note: True, they obeyed the Law, but the specific offering of birds signals their poverty.

options.text: They were wealthy and generous.
options.note: The opposite: the bird offering was the concession for the poor.

options.text: They were disobeying the temple regulations.
options.note: They were fully obedient; the birds were a lawful, prescribed offering.

quiz.q: What did Simeon say when he took the child in his arms?

options.text: His eyes had seen God's salvation, a light for the Gentiles and glory for Israel.
options.note: Verses 30 to 32 are the heart of Simeon's Nunc Dimittis.

options.text: He could now depart in peace as God had promised.
options.note: True and part of the song, but the reason is that he had seen God's salvation, the light for the nations.

options.text: The child would become a great teacher of the Law.
options.note: Simeon proclaimed Him God's salvation and light, far more than a teacher.

options.text: The child would drive the Romans from Israel.
options.note: Simeon spoke of salvation and light for all peoples, not a military deliverance.

quiz.q: What did Simeon foretell to Mary?

options.text: The child would cause the rise and fall of many and be opposed, and a sword would pierce her own soul.
options.note: Verses 34 and 35 give the warning of division and Mary's coming grief.

options.text: The child would be a sign that many would speak against.
options.note: True and stated, but it omits the piercing sword and the rising and falling of many.

options.text: Mary would become a great prophetess like Anna.
options.note: Simeon spoke of the child's mission and Mary's sorrow, not her becoming a prophetess.

options.text: Mary and the child would live a long, untroubled life.
options.note: He foretold opposition and a piercing sword, not an untroubled life.

quiz.q: Why did the boy Jesus stay behind in the temple?

options.text: Because He had to be in His Father's house, as He told His parents.
options.note: Verse 49 gives His own reason, revealing His unique sonship.

options.text: Because He was astounding the teachers with His understanding.
options.note: He did astound them, but His stated reason was that He had to be in His Father's house.

options.text: Because He had become separated from His parents by accident.
options.note: He remained behind purposefully; it was His parents who assumed He was with them.

options.text: Because He was lost and could not find the way home.
options.note: He was not lost; He was deliberately in the temple, His Father's house.

quiz.q: How does Luke describe Jesus' childhood after these events?

options.text: He was obedient to His parents and grew in wisdom and stature and in favor with God and man.
options.note: Verses 51 and 52 summarize His true human growth and obedience.

options.text: He grew strong and was filled with wisdom.
options.note: True and stated in verse 40, but the fuller summary adds His obedience and favor with God and man.

options.text: He immediately began His public ministry.
options.note: He returned to Nazareth and grew for years before His ministry began.

options.text: He stopped submitting to His earthly parents.
options.note: Verse 51 says the opposite: He was obedient to them.

questions: Simeon and Anna waited long years, faithful and prayerful, and were not disappointed. Where are you being called to patient, hopeful waiting on God's promises?
Simeon held the salvation of God in his own arms and could then die in peace. What would it mean for you to find your deepest peace in having seen and received Christ?
Jesus is a light for the Gentiles, salvation prepared in the sight of all peoples. How does the worldwide reach of the gospel shape the way you pray and live?
Simeon warned Mary of a piercing sword. How do you hold together the joy of following Christ with the real cost that discipleship can bring?
At twelve, Jesus was already drawn to His Father's house and His Father's business. Where is God inviting you to make His concerns your own?

translationNotes.note: The Child's father and mother describes Joseph and Mary in their ordinary roles; some later manuscripts read Joseph and His mother, likely to guard the virginal conception, but the meaning is unchanged, since Joseph was His legal father.
The BSB source places an em dash before and a sword will pierce your soul as well. In this edition the dash is rendered as a comma so the clause reads smoothly, without changing the sense.

In My Father's house is given in a footnote as about My Father's business; the Greek is compact, but either rendering points to Jesus' unique awareness that God is His Father and that He must be occupied with His concerns.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 2:22-52. Heading, line break, note, and poem markers were skipped when assembling verse text. The em dash in Luke 2:35 was converted to a comma; no em or en dashes remain.
